data.title: BlackBerry Is Finally Back (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Last week, we received fiscal fourth quarter results from BlackBerry (BB). The Canadian technology company has spent years evolving from a hardware focused firm to one aimed at software and services. The transition took much longer than expected, and shares lagged as a result. Finally, it appears that the story is back on track, which could mean future upside from here.

Previous coverage of the name

The last time I looked at BlackBerry was back in June 2025, right after the company had reported its fiscal Q1 results. While we saw nice headline beats, guidance was not that impressive, and key financial metrics showed some weakness. Since that time, BlackBerry shares have fallen by more than 21% (to Friday's close), compared to a nearly 12% rise in the S&P 500.

A look at the Q4 results

For the fiscal period that ended on February 28th, BlackBerry reported revenues of $156 million. This number was up 10% over the prior year period, and it handily beat street estimates by more than $11.4 million. The growth was driven mainly by a 20% increase in QNX segment revenue, as the automotive software package continues to grow nicely. Secure Communications, which had been a major drag in recent years, saw an 8% increase. Licensing revenues, which can be very lumpy, saw a more than 44% decrease but are a very small portion of the company's total.

On the margin front, BlackBerry saw a small increase for QNX margins but an 8 percentage point increase for Secure Communications. On a GAAP basis, operating income nearly doubled sequentially to $22.9 million, whereas the year ago period saw an $8 million loss that was aided by some non-recurring charges. Non-GAAP earnings per share came in at 6 cents, two cents ahead of the street, as BlackBerry almost always beats on the adjusted bottom line.

BlackBerry is also seeing nice improvement in some of its key metrics. The QNX royalty backlog was up $85 million in the past year to $950 million, a significant acceleration of growth. Three years ago, this value stood at just $640 million. Secure Communications annual recurring revenue also increased by $10 million after years of declines, with its dollar-based net retention rate edging up one percentage point to 94%.

Guidance and balance sheet update

For the current fiscal period ending in May, management guided to revenues of $132 million to $140 million, nicely ahead of the street's average estimate of $129.83 million. For the full fiscal year 2027, revenues are forecast to be in a range of $584 million to $611 million, compared to expectations of $577.25 million. QNX is again expected to be the growth leader, with the midpoint of segment guidance being 11.4% growth, with Licensing at 8.1% and Secure Communications at 6.2%. Adjusted earnings are forecast to be 15 to 19 cents per share, compared to 16 cents in the just-reported fiscal 2026.

Total cash and investments, including restricted cash, increased by $22.1 million year over year to $432.4 million. This was mainly due to free cash flow of $46.5 million, along with $38.1 million in proceeds related to the Cylance asset sale. Management spent $60 million on share repurchases for the year. BlackBerry has $200 million in convertible notes outstanding and is expecting to generate approximately $100 million in operating cash flow this year.

The current valuation picture

Based on Friday's close, BlackBerry's market cap stands at a little more than $2.25 billion. However, the stock is just 4 cents away from the price at which its convertible notes could convert to equity, so at that price, assuming conversion, the market cap would jump to $2.48 billion. Based on current street estimates, that would put the stock at a little more than 4.1 times expected sales. Many software and service names with much better growth profiles go for high single digits or well into double digits on a price-to-sales basis.

Currently, street analysts have a mixed view of the stock. Of the 8 analysts with a rating in the past 3 months, 6 have hold ratings, with one strong buy and one strong sell. The average price target of $4.81 represents a more than 25% increase from Friday's close, whereas in my previous article the average represented a 6% downside.

Final thoughts and recommendation

Last week's fiscal Q4 report from BlackBerry was quite solid, showing that the company's turnaround can finally be put behind us. Revenues are now growing at a nice clip, with improving key metrics, and guidance for the current fiscal year was very impressive. The bottom line has also swung nicely, with decent cash flow generation allowing for some share buybacks.

With a valuation that's towards the lower end of the software and services space, I'm hiking my rating on the stock to buy. Shares have fallen quite a bit since I last looked at BlackBerry, but the overall situation here is much improved. While I'm not calling for this name to get back to the low $20s where I first covered it over a decade ago anytime soon, I think some solid gains could be had if this growth story pans out.

data.title: Undercovered Dozen: Zscaler, Aeluma, Devon Energy And More
data.description:
The Undercovered Dozen is a weekly Seeking Alpha editor-curated series highlighting 12 articles on lesser-covered stocks from the previous seven days. We hope this provides ideas and inspires discussion among the community.

Today, we're looking at articles published between April 3 and April 9.

Take a look at what these less-covered stocks might hold for you. And please join the conversation below to share what you think: Are any of these worth following up on? And are there other undercovered ideas that you like?

Amazon Vs. Starlink: The $9 Billion Globalstar Deal That Could Change Everything

Julia Ostian | Hold

Globalstar (GSAT), a satellite communication company, had rallied on April 1st after the market by about 15% on the news from the Financial Times that Amazon (AMZN) might be weighing an acquisition. According to the news, Amazon is ready to pay almost $9 billion, although the news was not confirmed by either of the sides.

Even though it's still early to speculate on this specific acquisition, I found this piece of news particularly interesting. I've been closely following Amazon's satellite Project Leo (before Project Kuiper) for some time now, and I find this business segment of Amazon fascinating because, while Musk's Starlink has a major first-mover advantage, Amazon is probably the only company in the world that has the resources to catch up and to become a serious competitor for Starlink in the commercial segment, potentially expanding to government contracts.

Read more here.

Zscaler: A Generational Opportunity To 'Buy' AI Tailwinds At A Discount

Gary Alexander | Buy

The stock market's backlash against software stocks in 2026 has been one of the most surprising shifts in sentiment in recent memory. By and large, the selling pressure has been indiscriminate, with investors dumping any stocks that have the slightest whiff of potentially being disrupted by AI.

Cybersecurity software stocks, in particular, have been hard hit ever since Anthropic's Claude released more robust security features. Zscaler (ZS), which made a name for itself as a cloud-first security platform, has been especially hard hit, with its stock down nearly 40% since the start of the year. The question for investors now is, will the pain continue, or is this just a temporary slump?

Read more here.

TDVI: The Best Way For Income Investors To Invest In Tech

Austin Rogers | Buy

Unlike the manufacturing-driven economy of the mid-20th century, the US economy has become increasingly technology-driven.

That only becomes truer as population growth grinds toward zero, causing the economic weight to shift from the size of the labor force to productivity.

Little to no growth in the working-age population in developed countries, including the US, necessitates heavy investment into productivity enhancements.

As growth in the working-age population slows and reverses all across the developed world, productivity-enhancing technology has never been so important.

Put simply, today's income investor can't afford to sit out from tech.

That's where covered call ETFs focused on the technology sector come in. Several have been launched over the last five years or so.

The best of them, in my opinion, is the FT Vest Technology Target Income ETF (TDVI), which currently yields about 8%.

Let's dig in to this interesting tech-driven income opportunity.

Read more here.

Aeluma: Optical Bottleneck Platform Market Still Misprices- Supply Chain Bet, Not Science Bet

Prati Management | Strong Buy

While Lumentum and Coherent Corp. have rerated on NVIDIA’s optics demand, Aeluma (ALMU) remains treated as a pre-commercial science project at a ~$230M market cap.

That’s the setup. The market is pricing this like a science project. It’s not. It’s a sequencing error.

The consensus mistake is simple: investors are still underwriting device performance. The real constraint is packaging, qualification, and multi-fab portability.

This is not a bet on optical performance. It is a bet on which architectures survive contact with manufacturing reality.

Read more here.

Devon Energy: Merger Deal, Iran War, Price Tailwinds

The Asian Investor | Strong Buy

Devon Energy (DVN) is going to be a major beneficiary, like so many other oil and gas companies, from surging petroleum prices following America's and Israel's attacks on Iranian military installations, starting at the end of February. Although petroleum prices recently dropped on hopes of a peace agreement, U.S. President Trump recently threatened an escalating pace of military strikes on Iran again. As the risk of a prolonged military campaign in the Middle East is looming larger, I see U.S.-focused independent exploration and production companies like Devon Energy as strong investment choices for investors.

More importantly, Devon Energy just a short while ago announced a transformative merger with Coterra Energy, which will allow the energy enterprise to grow its production footprint, diversify more into natural gas, and potentially capture significant merger synergies.

Read more here.

Reinsurance Group of America: Baby Bonds Offer Solid Yield

Trapping Value | Hold

We have personally traded Reinsurance Group of America, Incorporated (RGA), multiple times during the last five years. At times, it has even been our largest holding. All of our trades have been on the long side, and our confidence in this business has been rewarded with rich dividends (figuratively and literally). As its name indicates, RGA is in the business of reinsurance, with a focus on life and health reinsurance products. Why do insurers need insurance?

Read more here.

Iovance Biotherapeutics: Positioned For Multi-Year Growth, Buy The Pullback

Jonathan Faison | Buy

Shares of Iovance Biotherapeutics (IOVA) have lost nearly two-thirds of their value since Amtagvi was approved in February 2024. On the other hand, shares were up by as much as 80% YTD mid-March due to increasing enthusiasm for Amtagvi's commercial prospects in melanoma along with accelerating clinical momentum plus improving margins (transitioning from science project to viable business).

Current pullback puts YTD gain at +20% and is likely due in part to aspiring competitor Replimune (REPL), with the April 10th PDUFA date coming up for RP1 in melanoma (combined with Bristol's PD-1 immune checkpoint inhibitor nivolumab).

While it's possible IOVA could experience a gap down if RP1 gets approved, I think bears and skeptics are missing the forest for the trees as the overall story continues to get better.

Read more here.

Applied Optoelectronics: Momentum Is Real, And Estimates Still Look Too Low

Cyn Research | Buy

Applied Optoelectronics, Inc. (AAOI) has been one of the stronger performers in the market this year, with the stock up nearly 3x while the S&P 500 is down more than 3%.

Applied Optoelectronics is a supplier of fiber-optic networking products. The company sells optical components, modules, and equipment used mainly in internet data centers and CATV broadband networks, with smaller exposure to telecom and FTTH markets. In 2025, 53.8% of revenue came from CATV and 42.9% from internet data centers, showing that AAOI is not a pure-play AI optics company but a broader optical networking supplier.

I believe AAOI still offers upside because the company appears to be executing well at a time when demand momentum is strengthening, and current estimates may still prove too conservative. In my view, the main opportunity today is not based on a long-term CPO thesis (which is valid as well but not part of this write-up) but on the chance that AAOI delivers faster revenue and earnings realization than the market is currently pricing in, leading to continued upside.

Read more here.

OXLC Is A Mess - Here's Why I'm Bullish

Bashar Issa | Strong Buy

Oxford Lane (OXLC) shares are up 20% in recent weeks after finding some support near the $8/share level. The stock price experienced steep declines in the past three months after write-offs, and the weakening portfolio hit its NAV/share.

In the previous article, I highlighted a potential market overreaction as the price discount to NAV surged. However, this opportunity should be viewed within the context of elevated risk. Although buying OXLC at a discount to NAV improves the chances of achieving positive total returns, those chances aren't particularly high to begin with. A significant portion of investors sit at a loss despite holding OXLC for many years. A better alternative is the company's notes and preferreds, such as OXLCG, the main focus of this piece.

Read more here.

DGRO: Buying The Best Sector Through The Best-Built Dividend Growth ETF

WLM Research | Buy

The iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO) seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of U.S. equities with a history of consistently growing dividends.

DGRO is the cleanest expression of the U.S. dividend growth segment available in an ETF wrapper with 403 names, an 8 bp expense ratio, and a Morningstar Gold Medalist rating. I am initiating DGRO on Seeking Alpha as a Buy. The fund is attractive on quality and construction, but room for multiple expansion is compressed without meaningful rate cuts or a sustained value factor rally, and I do not view either of these within the base case. What pushes this from a price-sensitive Hold to a Buy is the portfolio's allocated overweight to financials, which I view as the strongest sector in the market right now. I am willing to pay a higher multiple to access quality dividend growth with that sector tilt baked in.

Read more here.

Mid-America: A Dirt-Cheap Way To Buy Apartments

Nelson Smith | Buy

Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA) is an apartment REIT that owns class A assets in growing markets and that aren't encumbered with much debt. Excess supply and interest rate fears have caused the stock to fall, which is giving investors a nice opportunity to purchase the underlying apartments at an attractive discount to their true worth.

Read more here.

IDVO: A Covered Call ETF Driven By Hidden Momentum Factor

Andriy Blokhin | Buy

Amplify CWP International Enhanced Dividend Income ETF (IDVO) is one of the few active covered call ETFs that provides investors geographical diversification, foreign currency exposure and above average income. It could pair well with other US covered call funds to diversify income streams.

The fund has explicit focus on quality, income and momentum as its selection criteria. Momentum is especially important for IDVO. This has several important implications for the fund that I will delve into.

Read more here.

data.title: Wedgewood Partners Q1 2026 Top-Performing Contributors And Detractors
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Wedgewood Partners Q1 2026 Client Letter.

Top-performing contributors in the first quarter include Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), Alphabet (GOOGL), Chubb (CB), Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL), and Microsoft (MSFT). Top-performing detractors in the quarter include UnitedHealth (UNH), Copart (CPRT), Tractor Supply Company (TSCO), PayPal (PYPL), and CDW (CDW).

During the quarter, we bought Toll Brothers (TOL) and Progressive (PGR), sold PayPal, increased Amazon (AMZN), and trimmed Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing twice.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company was a top contributor to portfolio performance in the first quarter. Revenues grew +25%, and the Company guided to accelerating revenue growth to +30% in 2026 as demand for compute accelerators for AI applications continues to ramp unabated. In addition, the Company recently reported that March revenue was up +45% year over year, +31% month over month, and +35% year to date. The semiconductor customer base has evolved to the point that the Company increasingly works directly with non-traditional end customers, particularly cloud service providers, to develop custom silicon. This helps the Company better match supply with demand, so despite strong revenue growth, the Company has kept capital expenditures relatively in line with revenue growth. In addition, the Company is raising prices as utilization rates at leading-edge nodes continue to climb. We trimmed positions because our holdings exceeded 10% of portfolios. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company remains a top holding.

Old Dominion Freight Line also contributed to the quarter's performance. As in the fourth quarter of 2025, investors have grown more optimistic about a rebound in industrial activity, a core source of demand for Old Dominion's fleet, as U.S. manufacturing data suggests a nascent expansion is underway after more than three years of contraction. As usual, the Company has managed its capacity exceptionally well during this downturn, keeping costs under control and charging prices that reflect the longstanding, dependable service it provides its customers. Compared with peers, Old Dominion maintains a significant buffer of spare capacity, which helps capture more of the economics during an expansion. The Company has significant pent-up earnings growth ahead, which should attract new investors.

Motorola Solutions (MSI) contributed positively to performance, with organic revenue up 7% and orders growing in the double digits for the third consecutive quarter. The Company's core Land Mobile Radio (LMR) business offers a unique value proposition to its customers, particularly local governments and public safety agencies. LMR technology is well suited for inclement weather and harsh conditions because its antennas generate a high-powered signal, unlike competing modes of communication (e.g., cellular and satellite), which rely on clusters of low-power deployments and are more susceptible to degradation when power or network backhaul becomes unavailable due to power failures or deteriorating weather. In fact, Motorola devices can communicate radio-to-radio without a network if necessary. In addition, Motorola's command center software and new unmanned aerial communication platform (Silvus) are value-added services that public-safety customers are increasingly adopting to do more with the same personnel. We continue to hold Motorola as a core position in portfolios, having added to it in the fourth quarter of 2025.

Chubb contributed to performance during the quarter, growing core operating income by +25% on +8% growth in net premiums (FX-neutral) and achieving a record-low combined ratio. Despite a soft industry backdrop, particularly in property, Chubb grew new business by +17% in its core small and middle-market segment, and international global retail premiums were up by +13%, driven by distribution through large local financial intermediaries and a direct-to-consumer digital buildout. Underwriting has benefited from a quiet year on the "mega-cat" front, but the Company has also performed well during previous periods of higher mega-cat activity and is focused on driving margins by controlling overhead through several productivity initiatives. We expect Chubb to grow earnings per share and tangible book value per share at double-digit rates this year.

O'Reilly Automotive (ORLY) helped performance this quarter, as earnings per share grew by +13%, driven by a +6% increase in same-store sales (comps), marking the Company's 33rd consecutive year of positive comp growth. The primary driver of O'Reilly's quarterly comp performance was its professional business, which grew by over +10%. This was partially aided by tariff-driven inflation, which the Company successfully passed through to customers. Weather did not play a significant role, making this quarter a clear indicator of the Company's underlying fundamentals. Despite higher gas prices late in the first quarter potentially dampening consumer sentiment, we expect O'Reilly to continue posting solid comp and earnings growth, as the vast majority of its revenues are non-discretionary.

Microsoft was a leading detractor from performance in the first quarter. Despite revenue growth of +15%, operating income growth above 20%, and a backlog that more than doubled (to over $600 billion), the stock's multiple contracted to multi-year lows. Much of the initial negativity during the quarter centered on Microsoft's capital expenditures, which grew +65%. As we have shown in recent letters, Microsoft's incremental capital expenditures have yielded excellent returns over the past few years. So, while revenue "only" grew +15% compared to 65% capex growth, the more-than-doubling of the Company's backlog and the tripling of its commercial bookings are reasonable demand markers that suggest returns on invested capital from this capex cycle are likely to remain strong. Further, Microsoft is the largest shareholder in OpenAI, with a stake worth well over $100 billion based on recent fundraises, a fraction of Microsoft's $2.75 trillion market cap but close to 10X more than the Company's initial investments. Microsoft's capital allocation discipline and track record are sorely underestimated. Lastly, the market began discounting nearly all "seat-based" software stocks on the perception that they could be undermined by emerging AI workflows and competition. Rather, we believe investors are fading large-cap tech to make room for the mega IPOs later this year, particularly OpenAI, Anthropic, and SpaceX (which includes xAI). As these companies provide more disclosure and the market digests this impending glut of issuance, the long-term, complementary, and mission-critical (sticky) nature of Microsoft's productivity suite will become more evident.

Top IPOs Anticipated in 2026

Company Sector Est. IPO Valuation Exp. Timing
SPACEX Aerospace/Satellite >$1 Trillion Q3-Q4 2026
OpenAI Artificial Intelligence $830B - $1T Late '26/Early '27
ByteDance Social Media $480-500B Considering 2026
ANTHROPIC Artificial Intelligence $230B - $300B Preparing H2 2026
databricks Data/AI Infrastructure $134B - $160B Likely Q1-Q2 2026
stripe Fintech/Payments $91.5B - $120B Signalled H1 2026
Revolut Digital Banking $75B - $90B 2026 Target
Canva Design Software $50B - $56B Exp. H2 2026

AMPLIFYME

Click to enlarge

Booking Holdings (BKNG) detracted from overall performance during the quarter. Earnings per share grew +17%, with revenues up +16%, as travel demand remained strong late into 2025 and into early 2026. Most of the stock's weakness stemmed from investors labeling it an "AI loser" and, later in the quarter, the outbreak of war in the Middle East. Consumer AI, as a disruptive force in existing commerce, is proving to be much more difficult than markets expect. We view these AI tools and distribution channels as incremental rather than as drivers of the "zero-sum" dynamics that markets have been craving. As for the Middle East, for now, it represents a short-term disruption to travelers, particularly air travelers, given credible risks to international airline fuel supplies. However, we expect this will not be a multi-year headwind - more like a few quarters.

Meta Platforms (META) detracted from performance despite reporting fourth-quarter 2025 revenue growth of +24% and guiding to accelerating revenue growth of +30%, its fastest since 2021. The market began punishing stocks, such as Meta's, that are spending heavily on capex to fund AI-related infrastructure, regardless of the tremendous productivity gains they have exhibited - with headcount up just +6% on +24% revenue growth. The market further pressured the stock after oil prices spiked at the beginning of March. The assumption was that disposable income would be cannibalized at the pump, pressuring broader retail activity and related ad budgets. The forward earnings multiple on Meta has fallen to levels more consistent with much slower growth than the Company has guided to. After trimming Meta several times over the past few years, we are more likely to add to positions based on the strong Company-specific benefits that their long-term AI investments are yielding, given a valuation that is at a substantial discount compared to growth stocks and recent history.

Alphabet grew its core search revenue by +17% and continues to accelerate as users spend more time on Google properties, driven by the Company's AI offerings (AI Mode and AI Overviews). At the same time, Google advertisers are adopting more AI-enabled tools to improve ad relevance and accuracy. These AI gains are unlocking significant productivity and driving margin expansion across both core Google services and the $70 billion run-rate Google Cloud business. Given this massive operating leverage, an accelerating revenue profile, and a historic ability to generate high returns on invested capital, we believe their elevated capex spending plans this year are fully justified. We continue to hold Alphabet as a top weighting.

Visa (V) reported strong revenue and earnings-per-share growth of +15%, driven by solid consumer spending and the continued expansion of "value-added" services. Despite this fundamental strength, the market pressured shares, ostensibly due to potential regulatory and legislative headwinds. The White House recently backed a legislative amendment that could introduce incremental competition into the Mastercard/Visa duopoly in the United States. While a legal framework for competition is undoubtedly an incremental risk, we view the technological and practical execution of actual competition as far less of a threat. Furthermore, attempts to attach the amendment to recent legislation have failed, making it far from certain that this new competitive framework will see the light of day.

The information and statistical data contained herein have been obtained from sources that we believe to be reliable, but we do not warrant their accuracy or completeness. We do not undertake to advise you of any changes in figures or our views. This is not a solicitation of any order to buy or sell. We, our affiliates, and any officer, director, stockholder, or member of their families may have a position in and may from time to time purchase or sell any of the above-mentioned or related securities. Past results are no guarantee of future results.

This report includes candid statements and observations regarding investment strategies, individual securities, and economic and market conditions; however, there is no guarantee that these statements, opinions, or forecasts will prove correct. These comments may also include speculative opinions and should not be relied on as statements of fact.

Wedgewood Partners is committed to communicating with our investment partners as candidly as possible because we believe our investors benefit from understanding our investment philosophy, investment process, stock selection methodology, and investor temperament. Our views and opinions include “forward-looking statements” that may or may not be accurate over the long term. Forward-looking statements can be identified by words such as “believe,” “think,” “expect,” “anticipate,” or similar expressions. You should not place undue reliance on forward-looking statements, which are current as of the date of this report.

We disclaim any obligation to update or alter any forward-looking statements, whether as a result of new information, future events, or otherwise. While we believe we have a reasonable basis for our appraisals and have confidence in our opinions, actual results may differ materially from those we anticipate.

The information provided in this material should not be considered a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Eurofins: Predictable Growth At A Reasonable Price
data.description:
Investment thesis

Eurofins (ERFSF) might not be the type of company that immediately stands out, given its moderate growth. That said, I believe Eurofins offers a relatively predictable growth profile due to its stable business. If the company can deliver on its plan to grow organically at a mid-single-digit rate, along with acquisitions and returning capital through dividends and share buybacks, I would consider the current price a reasonable entry point.

Large-scale laboratory network

If you've ever needed a blood test to assess your health or confirm a diagnosis, you already have an idea of ​​the type of services Eurofins offers. But what I think sets Eurofins apart is its huge scale in providing these services.

By the end of 2025, the company had over 600 laboratories in Europe, around 200 in North America, 150 in Asia, and 20 in South America and had even entered the African continent, with two laboratories currently operating there. In my opinion, there's enormous growth potential in Africa due to the expected population boom. If the economy and society in general improve, healthcare systems and standards should also improve, and Eurofins could benefit because it already has considerable scale and experience to offer.

Scale is crucial in this type of business because it allows fixed costs like the medical equipment or specialized personnel to be spread across larger volumes of diagnoses, which should translate into better margins. Also, I consider that a larger scale can also build switching costs, because large pharma or food clients prefer to work with one single provider capable of offering global standards rather than dozens of small laboratories in different regions.

The type of analysis I mentioned at the beginning represents only 21% of Eurofins sales. The most important segment is what they call "Life" (42% of revenue), where they analyze food, water, and agricultural products to verify that the products are free of contaminants and comply with safety standards before reaching supermarkets. Next is the "BioPharma" segment with 28% of sales, where they basically support drug development. And the final 10% of sales come from the "Consumer & Technology Products Testing" segment, where they test materials and products to ensure they comply with safety and quality standards.

The fastest-growing segment at the end of 2025 was Life, with 6% organic growth. The difference between the two growth metrics is due to the company excluding acquisitions, divestitures, and currency effects in the calculation of organic growth in an attempt to isolate the true performance of the business. In any case, I think it's good to see that all segments are growing organically and that the largest segment is the one growing the most, because it means the company is betting on the right segment.

The effect of COVID-19

Something the company emphasizes in its investor presentation is that growth and profit margins have fully normalized after the impact of COVID-19. At this point, you might be wondering what something that happened in 2020/2021 has to do with the business now. Well, during that period, Eurofins experienced a surge in demand for testing services, particularly for COVID-19. And because capacity was limited at the time, the company was able to charge relatively high prices, and this brought very high profit margins too.

The EBITDA margins went from 20% before COVID-19 to peaks of nearly 28% in 2021. The problem is that this wasn't sustainable, and as COVID-related testing declined, those high-margin revenues disappeared, creating the illusion of weak growth and margin compression in recent years. But now that the business appears to be normalized, management anticipates organic growth of around 6.5% annually combined with some acquisitions from 2027 onwards.

We're still confident that we can revert to the typical organic growth we've had for decades of 6.5%, just to give a number, but mid-to high single digits. Gilles Martin - Chairman of the Board & CEO.

Valuation

While the board mentions organic growth of 6.5% annually, I'd like to see at least one year with that growth before adopting it as the baseline scenario, since at the close of fiscal year 2025, growth was only 5.0% with approximately 3.7% organic growth. Therefore, conservatively, I'll estimate 6% annual sales growth to allow for positive rather than negative surprises.

Now, I think the company could achieve free cash flow margins of 10% over time if it manages to reduce capital expenditures on new labs thanks to its economies of scale. If this is the case, then within five years it could earn almost €1 billion euros (this is the currency in which it reports) of cash flow.

With a P/FCF multiple of 20 times, which is reasonable for the industry and the stability of the business, the market cap would be almost €20 billion if bought on the Paris stock exchange, and in terms of per share, it would be more than €100 per share thanks to the fact that the company bought back almost 4% of the shares this year, lowering the share count to 186 million.

I’ll admit the 10% expected return here isn’t the most compelling at first glance, but I think it’s reasonable to accept a lower return profile for a business that is so stable and defensive. There’s also a 2% dividend yield that adds to the total return, which shouldn’t be ignored.

More importantly, I don’t think the base case tells the full story. If the company can deliver on its 6.5% organic growth target and an additional 1-2% through acquisitions, the return would then be around 12% + 2% dividend yield. And if they continue to buy back shares as they have in recent years, that could provide an additional shareholder return.

Risks

What strikes me the most is the company's reliance on acquisitions. In 2025 alone, they acquired 40 laboratory companies, and historically this has been a key driver of growth. However, it also implies that future growth depends in part on attractive acquisition targets and the discipline to pay only reasonable prices. If opportunities are scarce or valuations are high, growth could slow. And indeed, their 2025 presentation shows how laboratory startups have declined in recent years.

This relates to the balance sheet. Eurofins has used debt to grow, but this makes it vulnerable to financing conditions. If interest rates rise again and we experience an environment like that of 2022, borrowing costs could increase significantly, and the pace of acquisitions could be affected, as I think the priority would (or should) be to repay debt. So far, this hasn't been a problem, and debt maturities appear distant and well distributed, but it's something worth monitoring.

The bottom line

Eurofins is a typical business with moderate growth that might not immediately catch your eye, but it can be a good defensive portfolio holding with stable returns, avoiding major shocks along the way and consistently adding dividends. At the current price, it seems like a reasonable buying opportunity. The thesis is simple: a stable and kind of predictable business returning capital to shareholders could offer a potential double-digit return.

The Q1 2026 report is scheduled for April 19th, and I'd like to see signs that the management's guidance for the long term can be met. This begins with the promised mid-single-digit organic growth for FY2026 and margins exceeding those of 2025. If this happens, it's a good sign that execution is on track and the long-term goals are achievable.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Lyft: Short Squeeze Potential As Oil Crisis Could Trigger Rideshare Demand
data.description:
One industry that may benefit as a side effect of the Iran War oil/gas spike is the rideshare group. If gasoline prices spiral above $5-6 over the next few months, while used car prices and auto insurance rates continue their ascent higher (like 2025 and early 2026), many working-class individuals may give up on vehicle ownership, opting in favor of on-demand rideshare services and public-sponsored options of transportation.

A huge shift in travel choices will not be necessary to meaningfully jump demand for companies like Lyft, Inc. (LYFT). The good news is Lyft is very well run by management. It has a clean and cash-heavy balance sheet, was growing nicely before late February's military action in the Middle East, and just experienced a wicked share price decline since November. The end result of all these factors is LYFT is now available at a solid GARP (growth-at-a-reasonable price) valuation.

Reviewing management decisions of late - the push to expand globally especially into Europe, partnering with related travel-focused companies, and retaining a conservative low-debt approach to running the business - are standout positives for ownership.

While Uber (UBER) is the giant in the short-mileage passenger field, growth rates and profit margins could be peaking in 2026 and beyond. Robotaxis and autonomous entrants are grabbing market share, but Waymo-Google (GOOGL) (GOOG) vehicle and mapping costs are high, and Tesla (TSLA) efforts may have safety issues hard to resolve (using cameras vs. radar). No doubt the business model allure of self-driving vehicles (lacking driver wages) is real, but their rollout will still take many years. In my view, air taxis will be a decades-long project, where users will be paying dramatically higher rates for time savings. Consequently, eVTOL operators Archer Aviation (ACHR) and Joby Aviation (JOBY) remain speculative projects with no guarantee of success. Plus, they may never steal sizable market share from established companies in the industry.

All told, the Lyft business model, share valuation, and sustainable path to climbing sales and earnings (before any uptick in passengers from the 2026 oil shock) point to this single investment name as an obvious pull for my capital.

Let's review some of the logic for a bullish LYFT view going forward, especially if your holding target is less than 3-5 years.

The Business

Mirroring Uber, Lyft runs a simple to use app on your smartphone with various options for a ride to a destination in your area. Pricing is clearly explained and rides are available on demand in most city locations (with wait times measured in minutes).

Before gas prices started to rise, the average new auto owner in America was estimated to be paying between $8,000 and nearly $15,000 per year to operate a vehicle (including the cost to purchase/rent/lease a car, both regular and surprise maintenance, insurance & gasoline - over 15,000 miles per year, according to MoneyGeek).

If you average $30 for a two-way trip per rideshare, you would have to purchase 300 or more annually before the math doesn't make sense (shifting back toward ownership).

From personal experience, operating a used car with minimal maintenance and insurance still runs at least $6,000 yearly, no matter the miles driven. In this regard, the elderly and teenage groups of drivers are finding less reason economically to own a vehicle. If you and/or your spouse work from home, maybe owning one car vs. two is the correct course of action, from a financial perspective.

Another excuse for investor optimism: Lyft continues to add partnerships with other transportation-related companies like DoorDash (DASH) food delivery and United Airlines (UAL). Developing an ecosystem of use touchpoints is helping to normalize ridesharing in our society.

It's no surprise gross bookings for Lyft continue to rise at a robust clip, where +19% YoY in the December quarter was regarded as a "weak" showing (the raw number of rides in the quarter did fall to +11% YoY growth). What happens if dramatically more expensive gas prices turn off car ownership demand, while increasing the attractiveness of on-demand transportation services, at minimal cost as needed?

Strong Balance Sheet & Rising Margins

On the balance sheet front, Lyft is sitting on a "net cash" position of $520 million, while main competitor (and much larger in size) Uber held over $4+ billion in financial obligations at last report. I have drawn a comparison of net debt to competitors involved in the rideshare and expanding robotaxi/air taxi marketplace.

With stock price changes largely mimicking each other over the past few years, cash flow vs. net interest expense ratios seem to favor Lyft over Uber in my mind as an investor with a choice (56x vs. 23x). Another interesting data point is Lyft runs slightly higher gross margins than Uber (34% to 33%). Further, LYFT has moved from a cash-burn operation in 2023 to a projected solid cash earner in 2026. (My research goal is to figure out which name will benefit in a greater compounding way from unexpected gains in customer demand for existing rideshare services.)

Management is still projecting EBITDA margins to rise materially by late 2027. Higher margins on sharply higher sales is what every intelligent growth investor should be hunting for.

Short Interest - Turnaround Catalyst?

As a consequence of many of the mentioned variables, I am more drawn to the financial setup of Lyft, despite disappointing revenue growth in the December quarter.

Yet, Wall Street bears tend to disagree. Short sellers have jumped into borrowed and sold share positioning on the release of disappointing sales, ignoring management forecasts of 15% growth in bookings during 2026. Which brings us to the next discussion.

Record high short interest approaching 20% of outstanding shares in March is the next best reason to consider owning Lyft, especially if an oil crisis bump to industrywide rideshare demand appears. Short sellers seem overly confident LYFT's future is not as bright as larger Uber ($5.3 billion vs. $143 billion in equity market capitalization), although both companies were expecting some headwinds in 2026, using guidance from a month ago. Interestingly, Uber's short position has declined all the way down to 2.6% of outstanding shares.

However, with repeatable GAAP earnings likely staying positive this year (with similar results in 2024-25), the company is becoming less reliant on employee share compensation to fund growth (currently around 6% in awards/dilution as a percentage of equity market cap vs. 14% in 2022).

Cash earnings, excluding the cost accounting for stock awards and intangible write-downs, are projected to grow smartly with sales expansion in the coming years. So, LYFT is morphing into a sustainable business model, after years of high growth without much for actual earnings 3-5 years ago.

In my opinion, continued growth in sales, cash flow and earnings will cause short sellers to rethink a bearish position. With such a massive amount of future buying fuel in the tank (short covering potential), an unusual imbalance in share supply/demand dynamics cannot be ruled out. I am talking about any increase in growth rates beyond current projections at LYFT could cause a stampede of short covering, where aggressive order flow for buys far outstrips regular (or even reduced) selling volume. If this proves the future, a return trip to $20 or possibly $30 a share could be in the cards later this year.

Winning Valuation Setup

If you are searching for a growth stock with a low valuation, Lyft fits this definition well. Seeking Alpha's computer-sorted Quant Valuation Grade is sitting at an overall "A-" rating today. (Uber is valued at "C+" in comparison.) Using a laundry list of financial valuation datapoints vs. the company's own trading history and metrics available from peer equities, LYFT has plenty of stored upside potential in your corner as an investor.

When we include debt and equity totals at each organization, Lyft stands out vs. peer and competitors on core cash EBITDA generation. EV to forecast 2027 EBITDA of 7x is the lowest (positive) number in the group, and about HALF of the expected Uber calculation.

While EV to sales initially was priced similar to Uber five years ago, a very wide discrepancy has evolved over time. Today's EV to trailing sales number of 0.75x for Lyft is not the same as 2.9x for Uber. It's also only slightly above the early 2023 all-time low. If Lyft owns a better balance sheet with higher margins on sales to start and stronger growth rates as a possibility, this wide spread on revenues makes little sense.

When we look ahead to 2027 estimates for cash earnings (on reduced Wall Street expectations mind you), Lyft's 7.1x P/E projection really stands out vs. Uber's 16.4x. Others involved in ridesharing services are even higher for a valuation or not yet producing income.

If free cash flow is your top valuation idea for a business, Lyft is the winner, hands down, with a 20.3% trailing free cash flow yield on the current $13 share quote. Uber is at 6.5% for an equivalent yield, with other alternatives closer to breakeven or burning cash.

Final Thoughts

Insiders were net sellers of Lyft at prices closer to $20 last year. However, they have become net buyers over the last several months on the dip in price (boxed in gold below). This subtle bullish shift is quite positive in my opinion. I would rather own a company with net management/insider accumulation.

A final reason for building optimism, the trading chart breakdown in February looks to be running its course soon. Why? Stable prices seem to mask buying trends emerging over the last eight weeks. The 14-day Average Directional Index number is getting close to a multi-year low, indicating a stronger balance of buyers and sellers vs. early in the year, with less volatility.

All the while, both the Accumulation/Distribution Line and On Balance Volume reading have been trending higher. The chart price and momentum pattern is beginning to look like the January to April 2025 bottoming period (boxed in green). If this is true, buying on weakness in coming weeks may be the prudent course of action.

A confirming technical buy signal may appear once the 50-day moving average is surpassed for several days on the upside. Such a trend change is often the first sign that a rebound in price is beginning. For example, between late April and November last year, Lyft was able to double in price, following the 50-day MA crossover.

Important Investment Risks

Perhaps the main operating risk at this stage is a severe recession causes business travel and vacation-related rides to tail off significantly. If such plays out, it could negate many of the gains achieved from consumers ditching personal cars.

In my opinion, given a deep recession scenario globally, the appeal of cutting the huge expense owning/operating a car (with a bump in rideshare numbers the end result) should outweigh the drag on Lyft trip numbers from fewer travelers/vacationers.

The next most critical risk to consider is short-term in nature, where a major bear market on Wall Street drops pricing and valuations on all equities, including Lyft. The odds of this occurring are actually quite high in April-May. Yet, this may also open an even better long-term opportunity to acquire LYFT shares, in terms of valuation, timing, and compounding potential.

Price Targets

I am using a price of $9 or $10 as a worst-case scenario for Lyft, near the low quote of April 2025, just above the $8 number of May 2023. There should be good technical support in this area, assuming operating growth rates do not decline materially.

On the upside, a valuation closer to Uber argues for a $20 to $25 price later this year, all other variables remaining the same. And, if shorts cover en masse on an uptick in business trends, Lyft could approach $30 in 12 months, still well below the $66 price of March 2021.

Upside reward potential of +100% to +120% over 12-18 months seems to trounce downside projections (given rotten outcomes) of -30% to -40%. Weighing the pros and cons, I rate Lyft a Buy under $15, with Strong Buy territory pegged under $10.

data.title: NerdWallet: Revenue Growth Continues Despite Ongoing Organic Search Challenges
data.description:
I continue to remain bullish on NerdWallet (NRDS) as this founder-led company has continued to find new ways to diversify the business and grow revenue despite the ongoing AI concerns.

Since my last article in mid December, NerdWallet is down roughly 27% while the S&P 500 has remained flat.

Despite these lackluster results, I’m a believer that NerdWallet can survive the AI apocalypse and continue to help customers on their quest to provide financial knowledge. Let’s dive into the company’s recent financial results and new developments.

Combating Organic Search Headwinds

I think the decline in organic search due to AI is the biggest concern for the business which the company even mentioned in their Risk Factors section of their latest 10K filing:

The introduction and acceptance of AI-assisted technologies could further impact search engine relevance, causing declines in our ranking and decreased platform traffic, affecting our financial results.

It seems clear AI usage is ramping up and will likely increase especially as Millennials and Gen Z consumers look for financial information to help with their decision making. NerdWallet has a strong brand which is in their favor and the company has noted that conversion rates on LLM referral traffic is higher than traditional search and it’s growing which is certainly a positive for the company.

I also want to touch on what I believe is an important point that was mentioned on the company’s latest earnings call which is that these large LLMs, like Google (GOOGL) aren’t going to go out and create a bunch of relationships with banks and other financial service partners like NerdWallet is currently doing.

Here’s what NerdWallet’s CEO Tim Chen had to say about this point:

I think if you think about the scenario where you're trying to do some form of agentic shopping or LLMs are trying to get more integrated, there's kind of 2 obstacles you really need to think about. So the first is regulatory. For example, you can't get an insurance quote from someone without an insurance license. And so if you look across, for example, credit, insurance, mortgages and investing, they require licensing where institutions need deterministic and compliant outputs, not probabilistic answers.

Chen went on to state the second issue is that you need these banks, insurance companies, etc… to buy in. If an AI agent is shopping for an insurance quote, it’s easy to stop that AI agent with multi-factor authentication.

With these established relationships and strong brand equity NerdWallet remains in an excellent position to continue to help consumers and SMBs.

Risks

Outside of AI, within the company’s Risk Factors section the other key risk to the business is their dependency on their relationships with banks and other financial partnerships. Adverse changes in these partners' financial health or a drop in marketing spend could significantly hurt NerdWallet.

Next as I’ve stated in prior articles, competition remains fierce. NerdWallet is combating numerous new entrants, which include not only companies but individuals. For example, content creators on Instagram and TikTok are creating content around financial literacy. However, much of this content isn’t as trustworthy as NerdWallet’s so hopefully young consumers can see the benefit in NerdWallet’s information.

Financials

Before we discuss Q4 2025 results, I want to take a step back and review the company’s financial results over the last few years. As you can see, since going public back in late 2021, NerdWallet has steadily continued to grow revenue over the years:

Over the years, the company has been able to expand margins as total costs and expenses have come down and as you can see below this has resulted in increases to income from operations and net income:

Moving on to Q4 2025 results, the company had revenue of roughly $225 million, which is an increase of 23% compared to Q4 2024. Insurance brought in the most revenue at $81 million, an increase of 13% year over year. Next was Emerging Verticals, which brought in roughly $53 million, a 57% increase. Next was Loans with sales of $42 million, which was a big jump of 141% compared to 2024. Credit card and SMB products brought in roughly $23 and $27 million for the quarter, both down compared to the fourth quarter of 2024.

Management did note that rather than reporting five categories going forward they are going to consolidate down to two: consumer and SMB.

Management noted the company expects 2026 Q1 revenue in the range of $224 to $232 million and non-GAAP operating income in the range of $28 to $32 million. NerdWallet is expecting full year non-GAAP operating income in the range of $95 to $110 million.

NerdWallet investors can appreciate that the company continues to buy back shares as the company repurchased $51 million in the quarter. As you can see above this has led to a decrease of both basic and diluted share counts compared to 2024 and 2023.

NerdWallet continues to have a fantastic balance sheet as you can see below with a growing cash balance, no long-term debt and enough current assets to cover all of their current liabilities:

Valuation

NerdWallet has a valuation grade of a “B” at Seeking Alpha:

I maintain that price-to-sales is the best valuation metric for this company. As you can see in the graphic below the company's forward P/S ratio is down considerably compared to a few months ago, which makes the company feel more undervalued:

From another perspective, I've updated my reverse discounted cash flow model for NerdWallet. Using the latest free cash flow figure with a discount rate of 10% and a terminal growth rate of 2%, even by growing the business at a mere 1%, the stock would still be undervalued:

Most analysts believe NerdWallet can at least grow in the mid single digits for the next few years. From a ratings perspective, both Seeking Alpha and Wall Street analysts are predicting upside for the stock, as Wall Street has an average price target of $14.50 as you can see below:

Compared to my prior article, I believe the company has a much more attractive valuation as it appears NerdWallet also seems to be a victim of companies falling due to AI risks.

Conclusion

Despite ongoing organic search headwinds, I believe NerdWallet is doing enough to combat AI and that the company has enough brand equity to stay relevant when it comes to providing financial literacy and support to consumers and SMBs.

I believe NerdWallet appears undervalued and that given the company’s ample cash and strong balance sheet, the business has resources to not only combat AI but can find new ways to continue to grow revenue.

For long-term investors willing to take on some risk, I maintain that this founder-led company is worth considering at this attractive entry point.

data.title: Teva: The Market Is Pricing The Past, Not The Pipeline - Initiating Buy
data.description:
Introduction

Teva Pharmaceuticals (TEVA) is a global pharmaceutical company historically known as the largest generics manufacturer, built through acquisitions, including the $40B Actavis Generics deal back in 2016. This strategy, in turn, resulted in significant leverage and directly exposed it to generic price deflation and led to prolonged equity underperformance.

Under the current CEO, Richard Francis, Teva has repositioned toward more specialty branded medicines, moved toward complex generics and biosimilars, and innovation-led pipeline expansion. I believe this shift has begun to translate into financial performance, reaching $17.3B in 2025 revenue and robust 3-year consecutive growth. Key products that Teva markets are a) Austedo, b) Ajovy, and c) Uzedy, which have shown a robust sales growth during the last few years.

Furthermore, the company's key late-stage pipelines, such as Duvakitug (anti-TL1A), Olanzapine LAI, Anti-IL-15 (phase 2b), DARI, and emrusolmin, drive the next chapter for the company. I believe the investors are focused especially on Duvakitug's success.

Duvakitug - phase 3 blockbuster opportunity

Duvakitug is a human monoclonal antibody targeting TL1A being co-developed by Teva and Sanofi for ulcerative colitis (UC) and Crohn's disease (CD). TL1A is attractive because it sits at the intersection of inflammation and fibrosis, which are strategically important in inflammatory bowel disease, also known as IBD, especially in CD, where structuring and fibrotic biology remain an unmet need.

Induction data - RELIEVE UCCD Trial

Phase 2b induction data came from the RELIEVE UCCD trial. Data suggests that at week 14, in the UC trial, the clinical remission was 47.8% on high-dose duvakitug (treatment) vs. 20.45% in placebo, which means that the placebo-adjusted improvement was ~27.4% (with a p-value of 0.003, which is highly statistically significant).

In CD, endoscopic remission was 47.8% on high-dose duvakitug vs. 13% on placebo, for a placebo-adjusted improvement of 34.8% with p<0.001. Low dose also showed efficacy, though it was less robust. My read on the CD data is particularly interesting because TL1A agents are often discussed as potentially relevant where inflammation and fibrosis intersect. Also, this was described as the first randomized placebo-controlled anti-TL1A study in CD, which, in my view, adds strategic significance.

I note that the phase 2b trial demonstrated a compelling induction efficacy and durable maintenance efficacy out to around 58 total weeks of treatment in the induction responder population. Based on the currently disclosed data, I believe this is one of the strongest datasets in the TL1A class, although things can change over time with the advent of phase 3 (with more patients, the dataset may deteriorate).

Maintenance data: arguably the most important/recently announced de-risking event for TEVA

In February 2026, TEVA and Sanofi reported long-term extension maintenance data; patients who responded after the first 14-week induction phase entered the 44-week double blind maintenance period, with re-randomization to 450 mg or 900 mg SC every 4 weeks. Among the induction respondents, at 44 weeks of maintenance, UC clinical remission was 55% on 450 mg and 58% on 900 mg. For CD front, CD endoscopic response was 47% on 450 mg and 58% on 900 mg. The total exposure at that point was about 58 weeks, including induction and maintenance. I believe here, the maintenance dataset matters because it addresses the bare question of whether the phase 2 induction is just an early signal or if the benefit persists.

The disclosed data argue the benefit is durable, though I note carefully that this is a responder-enriched population, not an all-comer randomized maintenance dataset against placebo from baseline. As such, durability may look stronger. However, the exact real-world conversion from induction benefit to long-term treated population benefit remains an important phase 3 question.

In terms of safety, the phase 2b induction disclosure suggests that Duvakitug was generally well tolerated, with overall treatment-emergent adverse event rates similar to placebo (50% vs. 50%), and the reported AEs across UC and CD were below 5% in the disclosed summary. Furthermore, there were low anti-drug antibodies in induction.

Net-net, I believe Duvakitug seems like a high-value, late-stage immunology asset, and in my view, one of the most critical drivers of the “innovation-TEVA” narrative. I believe the main remaining questions are not whether the drug has pharmacological activity, but how durable, differentiated, and broad it is across different lines of therapy. Furthermore, whether phase 3 data will replicate the phase 2 trial's efficacy magnitude and how it will compete with the two other big pharma late-stage TL1A class agents (namely Roche (RHHBY)(RHHBF) and Merck's (MRK) agent). Sanofi disclosed in Jan 2026 that four phase 3 studies had commenced, two in CD and two in UC, as shown below.

Valuation

I derive a $38 target price based on the forward earnings multiples framework, based on the 2026E consensus EPS of ~$2.68, and applying a 14x PE multiple. The select multiple reflects a partial re-rating scenario, positioning TEVA between its historical classification as a leveraged generic manufacturer (~10-12x) and a higher-quality specialty pharma peer set (~16-18x). I believe this mid-cycle multiple is justified by tangible evidence of transformation, including reaccelerating growth in the innovative portfolio, namely Austedo and Uzedy, improving margin trajectory toward ~30% by 2027, and continued deleveraging (net leverage <3.0x). At the same time, I do not assume full re-rating given ongoing headwinds, including generic price erosion, IRA-related pricing pressure on AUSTEDO, and residual legal liabilities.

Risks

Key risks for TEVA are pipeline failure risk, margin expansion to 30% (IRA can compress margins), and balance sheet risk - legal liabilities still seem to persist (namely, the opioid settlement, antitrust litigation, and EU fines).

Conclusion

I initiate Teva with a buy rating with a $38 target price based on a) clear transformation underway, b) visible earnings growth from the branded portfolio, and c) significant pipeline optionality, especially the duvakitug, which looks like a highly promising asset. I believe the risk still remains, including generic erosion, regulatory pricing pressure, and pipeline execution; however, with the multiple upcoming catalysts, I believe the risk-reward is skewed favorably.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The War Premium Vs. The Yield Premium: Which Force Will Seize Control And Drive Gold's Next Move?
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Gold prices experienced a rollercoaster start to the week with Hormuz inflation risks front and center after the US and Iran failed to reach a deal in Pakistan over the weekend.

The precious metal continues to struggle for a clear direction as two opposing forces, namely, the rising Middle East tensions and a hawkish shift in the Fed's outlook, tug at the precious metal.

Despite a recovery since the initial post-war selloff, the "safe-haven" rally appears to be losing its legs as traders weigh the risk of a regional conflict against the reality of "higher-for-longer" US interest rates.

The Geopolitical Floor: Hormuz and the Iran Deadline

The primary driver keeping gold from a deeper retracement is the simmering tension between the US and Iran. With the deadline for US-Iran mediation approaching, the market remains on edge.

Reports of a potential blockade of the Strait of Hormuz have sent shockwaves through the commodities sector, primarily boosting oil prices but also providing a fundamental "ceiling" for gold as markets fear the higher-for-longer rates narrative.

The downside is limited by the hopes of a potential deal, given President Donald Trump's record of "TACO". This is keeping the precious metal supported for now and sort of in a state of limbo until some clarity on the US-Iran situation comes to fruition.

The Macro Headwind: Fed Outlook and a Resilient Dollar

The Federal Reserve continues to act as the primary headwind. Recent US economic data has been uncomfortably resilient, forcing markets to rethink the timing and depth of rate cuts for 2026. This hawkish repricing has breathed new life into the US dollar and pushed Treasury yields higher, making non-yielding assets like gold less attractive.

From a macro perspective, the "war premium" is currently being offset by the "yield premium." As long as the US economy continues to defy gravity, the Fed has little reason to pivot, and that remains the single biggest hurdle for gold's path toward the psychological $5,000 mark.

Key Takeaways

Given the factors discussed above, gold is in a state of limbo, and this looks set to continue. The only way the current narrative shifts is with some sort of resolution regarding the Iran conflict.

If mediation fails and the Hormuz blockade becomes a reality, we could see a vertical move as the market prices in a massive inflationary shock, and higher rates could send gold prices tumbling.

Conversely, if a deal is struck, expect the "fear premium" to evaporate quickly, but gold could benefit as markets may view the inflation situation to be (for lack of a better word) transitory.

For now, gold is a passenger to the headlines. Traders should keep a close eye on the US Dollar Index (DXY) and energy prices, as these will be the primary leading indicators for the next major leg in the yellow metal.

Technical Outlook - Gold

Looking at the charts, gold's recovery is entering its third week, but the momentum is visibly waning. The price is oscillating within a tight range, finding immediate support at the $4,700 psychological level.

While the 100-period SMA (blue) has transitioned from resistance to dynamic support near $4,606, the upside remains capped by the descending 200-period SMA (orange) currently hovering around $4,870.

The RSI sits neutrally at 51.84, confirming a lack of directional momentum. Bulls need a decisive break above the recent swing high at $4,804 to challenge the $4,870 SMA and the elusive $5,000 handle. Conversely, a breach of the $4,700 support could see a swift retracement toward the $4,600 confluence zone.

Key Levels to Watch:

Resistance: $4,804, $4,870, $5,000

Support: $4,700, $4,606, $4,500

Gold (XAU/USD) One-Hour Chart, April 13, 2026

data.title: Look At The Starz, Look How They Shine For You
data.description:
Starz (STRZ) is a cable/streaming channel that focuses on premium programming for women. About 70% of subscribers are women, with much programming like Power and Outlander targeting a female audience. I believe the stock trades at an extremely compelling valuation with several upside drivers, including a potential buyout.

STRZ has about 17.6 million US subscribers. As you can see below, the OTT (streaming) subs have been growing at a nice clip while the linear subs have been declining. OTT is now about 72% of the total US subs, and grew 7.5% year over year and 3% sequentially. Linear is ~28% of subs and declined about 16%. The nice news about that math is that we are nearing an inflection point where OTT is at the size where its lower percent growth rate can overcome linear's higher percent growth rate in absolute terms.

The company reorganized their Canadian operations into a pure licensing arrangement that gets reflected on the linear and other revenue lines on the income statement. It's a small part of the business, and that move allows management to focus on US operations.

Revenue has been split at almost the same ratio as subscribers. In the nine months ended Dec 31, revenue was about 68% streaming vs. the rest linear and other (other includes Canada).

The company does not break out OIBDA (EBITDA with stock comp added back) by source (OTT vs. linear), and quarterly results have been pre-split, and immediately post-split have some items that won't appear going forward. However, this most recent quarter was largely clean, and EBITDA came in at $55.5 million.

You cannot completely annualize the December quarter results because of the timing of expenses and promotions, but the $204 million of OIBDA for 2025 slightly exceeded the company's $200 million guidance. Moreover, by producing its own shows, it reduces the programming amortization line item (effectively the cost of licensing the content from Lionsgate). The company believes that a combination of subscriber/revenue growth and this lower expense from in-house production can increase OIBDA from the $200 million range in 2025 to $300 million in 2028.

Performance as a Separate Entity

STRZ stock has underperformed since the separation from LION for what I believe are a few reasons:

  • It is a small cap (under $250 million market value) with limited liquidity, which limits the pool of potential investors.
    • The float is even smaller, with the two top holders owning about 28% of the shares. More on that below.
  • It is viewed more as a linear channel than a streaming service despite the subscriber data stated above. Thus it is viewed as a melting ice cube.
  • While STRZ has multiyear contracts for its hit shows, Lionsgate owns the content library. Many investors only want services that own the IP.
  • Limited time as a standalone company. Spinoffs and corporate separations often need time to gain traction as separate entities.

I think these items are largely why STRZ trades cheap on a relative and absolute basis, and I will tackle them one by one.

Small Cap

That's a bit of a chicken and egg issue. Part of why the stock is cheap is because it's a small cap, but part of why it's a small cap is because it's cheap. While it may seem counterintuitive, some investors wait until companies are higher valued/higher market caps before they play. The trick is to have the capital/disposition where you can tolerate the volatility that comes with lower liquidity/smaller cap shares.

On the large holder front, Steve Mnuchin just sold his 10.7% stake for $13.85/share to Byron Allen, who owns several media properties, including the Weather Channel. Allen has had mixed results as a buyer and owner of media assets. What matters, though, is that I have not seen a situation where Allen owns passive, minority stakes in anything. Only whole companies. There were two notable developments that occurred after this transaction. First, Allen filed a 13D, which implies he is not a passive investor. Second, in response to Allen's 13D, the company put a poison pill in place. LION put a poison pill in place when it first started trading as a separate company. The stated goal of that pill was "to protect shareholder interests, ensure fair value in any change-of-control transaction, and uphold the board's fiduciary duties." This is standard language to force a hostile bidder to negotiate rather than say the company is not for sale. I think it's reasonable to believe that STRZ's board has similar goals.

Linear vs. Streaming

STRZ is the only example I can think of that has transitioned the bulk of its subs from linear to streaming. As you can see in that table above, this dynamic continues. The company gained 370k net streaming subscribers this past quarter. The slate appears good to keep current subscribers happy and sign up new ones. The Spartacus revamp has been well received. The last season of Outlander just premiered. The Outlander spin-off, Blood of My Blood, premiered last summer. Its sequel will come later this year, as will Fightland, the STRZ's first series it produced on its own in partnership with 50 Cent. I think the melting ice cube misperception will dissipate over time as revenue and subscribers grow, which, most importantly, will grow profits.

Content Library

Fightland hopefully doesn't just mean a higher margin. It can also ease the concern around the lack of a library. This concern is not just with equity investors. Even though the company finished December with 2.9x net debt to OIBDA, which normally translates to at least a BBB rating, the debt ratings agencies were going to give the company's debt a CCC rating (low junk) because in the event of something like a global hack where subscribers could not access the service for an extended period of time and dropped their subscriptions, lack of library meant low recovery. The company chose to go unrated and expects to finish 2026 with 2.7x net leverage. For those of you who can buy the STRZ 2029 bonds, I think they're an excellent risk reward in the mid-80s.

Limited Time as Public Company

One can't make time move faster. I do think that most shareholders who didn't want to own STRZ shares post the separation have had the opportunity to sell.

Valuation

Here is where I get excited (and perhaps what caught Byron Allen's attention). STRZ is cheap on a relative and absolute basis.

Market Cap (@$14.57) $244 million
Debt $625 million
Cash $35 million
Enterprise Value $834 million
EV/2025 OIBDA ($204mm) 4.08x
EV/Run Rate OIBDA ($222mm) 3.76x
Unlevered FCF Yield ($60mm midpt) 24.5%

These numbers compare favorably to AMC Networks, the only other independent streaming service and linear channel. AMC has more revenue than STRZ but reports only 10.5 million streaming subscribers. It does not report linear subs. AMC has a small IP library. It owns the Walking Dead franchise as well as rights to Anne Rice's literary works (Interview with a Vampire, etc.) and a few others like Gangs of London. Other than Walking Dead, none of those series strike me as major hits with major followings.

Yet, despite fewer OTT subs, more debt (~5x leverage), and just a tiny library, AMC trades at around 5x EBITDA. If STRZ were to match this multiple on just 2025 OIBDA, the stock could more than double.

In addition, on a absolute basis, I consider any company that is growing and still trading around 4x EBITDA and above a 20% FCF yield to be extremely cheap on an absolute basis. My guess is Byron Allen agrees with me.

Risk

The main risk here (beyond a global hack that leads to massive subscriber losses) is that subscribers, particularly OTT subs, start bleeding. That can happen from subscribers turning against the content, the service not pricing right, or a number of other reasons. The other risk is Fightland is a major flop. In that scenario, the company is out the cost of production and has a hole in its content.

Conclusion

I think STRZ is a unique asset that trades cheap on a relative and absolute basis and has multiple catalysts to create value. The fastest would be a bid from Byron Allen to buy the whole company. Should that not materialize, free cash flow generation and building a content library of its own can create enormous value in the intermediate term. I think the shares can easily double from here with room for more gains.

data.title: One Streak Ends, Others Make History
data.description:
Over the weekend, ceasefire talks fell apart, and the Strait of Hormuz started facing a blockade this morning. Despite these negative catalysts leading to a negative tone at Monday's open, equities rallied throughout the session, with the S&P 500 finishing up over 1%.

That return to green candles also meant the S&P 500 has now rallied in eight of the last nine sessions. In fact, Friday was the only down day recently, and that snapped a seven-day winning streak.

As shown below, that was the longest streak of consecutive up days since another seven days last October. Going back to 2000, that was the 29th example of a winning streak of 7 days or more, with the longest going for nine days last May.

Again, winning streaks lasting at least 7 days have been somewhat uncommon. Filtering out those streaks to only look at those without another one in the prior six months, as was the case recently, there have been 48 instances since the start of the five-day trading week in 1953.

As shown below, from the close of the first down day that ended those winning streaks, the S&P 500's average performance has been modestly stronger than the norm and consistently positive in the weeks and months ahead.

The broad index isn't alone in stringing together a number of up days in a row. Of all S&P 500 members, seven are currently on even longer winning streaks of at least nine trading days, with one stock, State Street (STT), now on a ten-day winning streak.

In the charts below, we show how those streaks stack up versus their respective histories. None of these winning streaks has gone on long enough to be records, although STT and Microchip (MCHP) are each only one day away.

We would note that most of these stocks, other than STT, are in the tech sector or adjacent to it. Regarding Intel (INTC), its winning streak is one of many that have lasted 9 days throughout its history, but as we discussed in an earlier post, there hasn't been a time since 1984 that it has risen by more in such a span.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
